fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: We start with a flexi cap fund because it gives exposure to large, mid, and small companies across sectors. This fund has performed well over 5 years and charges a low yearly fee. It's from a trusted fund house and helps diversify your risk.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund invests in top 500 US companies like Apple, Microsoft, and Amazon. It’s low-cost and brings global diversification to your portfolio. Since you lived in the US, this connects you to a market you may feel more familiar with.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Large & Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund balances large companies (stable) with mid-sized ones (higher growth). It has a strong long-term record and helps grow your money steadily without taking extreme risks.

fund.name: WhiteOak Capital ELSS Tax Saver Fund Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: ELSS funds qualify for tax savings under Section 80C. You're in the 30% tax bracket, so this saves you up to ₹46,800 per year. This fund is equity-taxed and has a 3-year lock-in, but historically delivers good returns. We use SIP + lumpsum to use up your annual 80C benefit efficiently.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Coin by Zerodha if you don't have one. It takes 10 minutes with your PAN and bank details.
Step 2: Start your monthly SIPs of ₹40,000 across the four funds as mentioned.
Step 3: Make a one-time investment of ₹6 lakh in the ELSS fund to claim full 80C deduction this year.
Step 4: Set calendar reminders for SIP dates and review meetings every 6 months.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Check if all SIPs ran and if any fund is underperforming peers.
After 1 year: Review tax-saving options again and adjust ELSS if needed.
Every 3 years: Recheck your risk comfort as you near retirement.
If any fund consistently ranks in bottom 20% of its category for 2 years, consider replacing it.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check if your portfolio has drifted more than 5% from the target (40% Flexi, 20% US, 20% Large & Mid, 20% ELSS). For example, if ELSS grows to 28%, sell some and move money to underweight funds. This keeps your risk steady.
plan.rationale: At 45, returning to India after years abroad, you need a portfolio that balances growth and caution. You have 17 years to retirement and want a ₹5 Cr corpus. Your ₹45 Lakh existing savings plus ₹40k monthly SIP over 17 years can reach that goal if we earn around 13% yearly. We include US stocks for global exposure and ELSS for tax savings — smart moves for someone in the 30% tax slab. The mix of flexi, large & mid, and tax-saving funds offers steady growth without wild swings.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will go down temporarily. This is normal and usually recovers over 2-3 years.
The US fund depends on the dollar-rupee exchange rate. If the rupee strengthens, your returns may reduce.
ELSS has a 3-year lock-in. You can't withdraw that money early without penalty.
Inflation may rise faster than expected, reducing your real purchasing power at retirement.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

allocation_schedule.trigger: Initial setup: High equity for growth, ELSS for tax saving
At age 55, reduce risk gradually by adding debt and gold
Nearing retirement, shift to more stable options

aps.reasoning: passive_instrument_fraction: Only 20% (S&P 500 Index) of the 80% equity sleeve is passive; the remaining 60% is in active categories (Flexi Cap, Large & Mid Cap, ELSS). 20/80 ≈ 0.17. turnover_score: Guidelines call for annual rebalancing only when drift >5%, plus 6-month observation checkpoints; that is buy-and-hold behaviour → 0.75. cost_emphasis_score: Expense ratios are mentioned as ‘low’ once, but selection is driven by past return numbers and tax benefit, not cost minimisation; no push for the cheapest options → 0.3. research_vs_cost_score: Rationale highlights 5-year past performance, fund-house trust, and category blend; no mention of index philosophy or ignoring manager skill → 0.3. time_horizon_alignment_score: Portfolio is explicitly designed for 17-year horizon, with glide-path shift only every 5-10 years; very long-term view → 0.8. portfolio_activeness_score: Three of four funds are active equity categories with no evidence of closet-index behaviour; only 25% of the total portfolio is passive index exposure → 0.25.

pqs.reasoning: goal_alignment: The 17-year horizon and ₹5 Cr retirement target are explicitly mapped to a 13 % CAGR need; the 80 % equity start, 10 % SIP step-up and glide-path that begins de-risking only at 55 fit a 45-year-old moderate investor. Missing minor detail: no discrete planning for the 8-year school-fee cash-flow, but the SIP+existing corpus handles it implicitly. diversification: Good spread across Indian flexi-cap, large-&-mid, US S&P 500, and an ELSS (effectively another diversified equity bucket). All suggestions are equity; no debt, gold or geographical Asia fund, so concentration risk remains. risk_return_appropriateness: 13 % base case is realistic for an 80 % equity blend; bull-bear spread (9-17 %) is sensible for moderate risk; glide-path shown; risk warnings included. internal_consistency: Allocations add to 100 %, rationale matches funds chosen, rebalancing rule is clear, projected maths coherent, no contradictions. tax_efficiency: Recognises 30 % slab, places 20 % into ELSS to exhaust 80C, prefers equity-taxed vehicles throughout and explains lock-in and saving amount—no slab-taxed debt suggested.
